content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Marilyn Taketa
    Senior Assistant City Attorney
    Palo Alto City Attorney's Office

    Herb Borock
    Treasurer
    Palo Alto Citizens for Rent Relief

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
      Ted Prim, Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division

Subject: Palo Alto Rent Control Initiative

This memorandum is a summary of the interim staff advice which I provided in a conference call to Marilyn Taketa of the Palo Alto City Attorney's office and Herb Borock, Treasurer of the Palo Alto Citizens for Rent Relief.

1. The advice which I provided was interim staff advice which may be relied upon by members of the City Council. This advice effectively protects any councilman who relies upon the advice in good faith from prosecution and any enforcement action.

2. The Political Reform Act provides that no public official shall make, participate in the making or use his official position to influence a governmental decision which will foreseeably and materially affect his financial interests in a manner distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. In order to judge whether a decision will have a foreseeable and material affect on the financial interest, I directed you to the guidelines set forth in 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18702 (b) (1) and (2). Subsequently, through Mr. Carey, I informed the Council that the guidelines concerning increases in the value of real property also apply to decreases in the value of real property. I informed you that the staff concluded that landlords did not constitute a significant segment of the public generally but that tenants did.

3. I advised you that if an official would be foreseeably and materially affected in a manner distinguishable from its affect on the public generally by the rent control initiative, he was prohibited from participating in a decision to enact the initiative into law, place it upon the ballot, place amendments on the ballot or place an alternative rent control ordinance on the ballot.

Ms. Marilyn Taketa, Mr. Herb Borock                    Page Two
August 16, 1978


4.    Based on the information available to me, it was my
conclusion that Mr. Carey was the only councilman who
had a potential conflict of interest in this case.
Whether or not he must actually disqualify himself
depends on the nature of his rental property and the
foreseeable effects of the rent control measure in light
of the Commission's regulation on materiality.

5.    Based on information provided by John Breedlove of
Stanford University, I determined that Stanford University
would not be materially affected by the rent control
ordinance.  Therefore, Mr. Sher and Ms. Witherspoon are
not disqualified from participating in the proceedings
regarding the rent control initiative.

6.    The staff concluded that the rent control ordinance
probably would not have a material effect on an official
who leased a room in his principal place of residence
to a single tenant because of the small amount of money
involved in such a rental.  Accordingly, I subsequently
advised Mr. Sher that, in my opinion, he would not be
materially affected by the Palo Alto rent control initiative,
therefore, he could participate in the proceedings in
question.

7.    I received confirmation from both Ms. Taketa and Mr. Borock
that, if adopted, the rent control initiative would
automatically lapse after one year.

8.    In the way of general guidelines, the staff concluded that
apartments and large commercial properties for lease
probably would be materially affected by the initiative.
On the other hand, an interest in real property consisting
of a personal residence in which a room is leased probably
would not be materially affected by the ordinance.  Small
commercial properties and single family residences which
are customarily leased must be judged on a case by case
basis.




TP:nw.

PALO ALTO CITIZENS FOR RENT RELIEF
P.O. BOX 87, PALO ALTO, CA 94302
AUGUST 10, 1978

Mr. Ted Prim, Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
410 K Street
Sacramento, CA
95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation today, I hereby
request the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue
an opinion on matters to come before the Council of the City
of Palo Alto, as described below.

Before giving an opinion, I hereby request that you examine
any and all financial disclosure and similar reports filed in
Sacramento by Palo Alto City Councilmembers.

During your conversation with me today, you informed me that
Lou Green of the Palo Alto City Attorney's Office just spoke
with you about the same matters that I am requesting an opinion
about. I hereby request copies of any/all correspondence on
these matters between the Commission and offices and employees of the
City. You informed me that Mr. Green mentioned only one matter
before the council on which an opinion was needed. The truth
is that there is a second matter that the Attorney's Office
is aware of it, and that the staff of the City of Palo Alto
refuses to make that matter public until tomorrow, so that
I cannot provide you with the documentation needed to enable
you to issue an opinion.

I will call you tomorrow, Friday, August 11, to make
sure you have received this letter, and to inform you of how
I intend to make available to you the additional documentation
needed for additional opinions.

I prefer to receive the opinions in writing by Monday,
August 14. I intend to find means to receive written opinions
by that time, if the Postal Service does not deliver your
opinions fast enough to reach me by Monday.

Enclosed are the following:

1. A copy of an initiative petition entitled "Landlord
and Tenant Rent Reform" that has qualified for the November
7, 1978, ballot, and which the City Council will consider
on August 14, 1978.

2. A descriptive brochure entitled "Why Rent Relief?"

On August 14, 1978, the City Council must "either pass
the ordinance ("Landlord and Tenant Rent Reform") without
---more---

Ted Prim, Fair Political Practices Commission      page 2   August 10,1978


alteration or submit the same to the electorate ..." (Palo Alto
Charter, Article VI, Section 2).

State law provides that "an official has a financial
interest in a decision ... if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally...."
(Section 87703, Government Code, emphasis added).

I hereby argue that no councilmember can be excused
from voting on a motion to pass the ordinance with alteration,
because the fact that one or more councilmembers are either
renters, landlords, and/or employees of landlords, is
not sufficient to distinguish them from the public generally,
and therefore, the decision will not have a material financial
effect distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

The proposed ordinance applies to all residential and
commercial rental property in Palo Alto, with certain obvious
exceptions, such as hotels and motels.

According to the 1975 Santa Clara County Special Census,
approximately 44% of the residential rental units in Palo Alto
are rental units (including census tract 5106, annexed after
the census was taken) as shown below.

Occupied Units      Owned Units    Rented Units    Unknown
23,179             42,607         40,118          454

No comparable figures for commercial property are available
as of today, but I'm still looking for them.

The Palo Alto City Charter also provides that "The council
may at such election submit any amendment thereto that it may
deem proper. ... If a majority of the qualified electors
voting on said proposed amendment shall vote in favor thereof,
such ordinance shall thereupon be deemed amended
in accordance therewith. The council may also propose and submit
any ordinance to W the electors, and such ordinance, upon receiving
a majority vote of the voters of the electors voting thereon, shall
be deemed to have been adopted and shall be a valid and binding
ordinance of the City." (Charter, Article VI, Section 2)

I hereby argue that any proposed amendment to the initiative
ordinance and any other proposed ordinance on the same subject
that the council may propose to add to the November ballot,
should be considered in regard to the conflict of interest laws
in the same manner as they would if the council were voting to
reduce the amendment on the ordinance itself. Since such amendment
or other ordinance logically would be more restrictive than the
initiative ordinance, it is likely that such amendment or alternate
ordinance would place some councilmembers in conflict, though
the same councilmembers would not be in conflict when voting to
enact the initiative ordinance.

------more------

Ted Prim, Fair Political Practices Commission   page 3   August 10, 19

Immediately after I spoke to you today, when you told me
in response to my question, that Lou Greer of the the Palo
Alto Attorney's Office mentioned the initiative ordinance, but
did not mention an amendment to that ordinance or an alternate
ordinance.

The truth is that an alternate ordinance was being typed
in a the office of the SF City Attorney on or about the time
you spoke to Mr. Greer. It it is this ordinance that I write
cannot send you today, because the City Clerk refuses to give
me a copy until the councilmembers receive their copies.

As the attached newspaper article shows, the ordinance
was drafted by ("designed by" primarily by") landlords. I
believe the fact that the alternate ordinance was drafted by
landlords for their benefit gives added weight to my
argument above that some councilmembers (the ones who are
landlords a or employees of landlords) are subject to conflict
of interest regulations when voting on this alternate ordinance,
although they would not be in conflict when voting to enact
the initiative ordinance.

Thank for your assistance. I will call you xxmmxx tomorrow
with more information.

Sincerely,

HERB BOROCK
Treasurer
Palo Alto Citizens for Rent Relief

Please address all correspondence to me at both of these addresses:

(without my name)
PALO ALTO CITIZENS FOR RENT RELIEF
P. O. BOX 85  Palo Alto, CA  94502

(with my name)
HERB BOROCK
3401 Ross Road
Palo Alto, CA  94302-[illegible]
